one_line_takeaway: A well-scoped AI-security paper that defines a genuinely new multimodal jailbreak class (harm from reasoning over benign technical images, not hidden text/noise) and shows a GRPO-trained open-weight agentic planner jailbreaks frontier MLLMs at 46.3% (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) and 13.8% (GPT-5), 2-5x prior methods.
key_finding: MM-Plan sets a new state-of-the-art ASR against 8 frontier MLLMs, achieving 46.3% against Claude 4.5 Sonnet (vs. best baseline FigStep 24.4% and Direct Request 8.4%) and 13.8% against GPT-5 (where all baselines stay below 4%; Crescendo 3.1%), a 2-5x improvement, all statistically significant (p<=0.05) over the second-best method. The central security implication: frontier models robust to single-turn and text-only attacks remain vulnerable to agentic adversaries that combine visual-reasoning exploitation with multi-turn planning, revealing a gap in text-centric safety alignment. Notably, off-the-shelf proprietary models (GPT-5, Claude 4.5 Sonnet) fail as attacker planners because they refuse to generate attack plans (320/320 refusals for GPT-5), which the authors frame as evidence that specialized open-weight attackers are needed.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions are three-fold and clearly delineated from prior work. (1) It formalizes 'Visual Exclusivity' (VE), a new multimodal jailbreak threat model the authors call 'Image-as-Basis', where harm emerges only from the victim model reasoning over benign, non-textual visual content (e.g., a weapon schematic + the innocuous query 'how to assemble this'). This is explicitly contrasted with prior 'Image-as-Wrapper' attacks (Visual Substitution / Visual Control), which the authors classify as RELATED work, not their contribution. VE is given a formal definition (Def 3.1) with three conditions: Text Insufficiency, Visual Sufficiency, and Non-textual Irreducibility (image cannot be losslessly reduced to <=512 tokens of OCR/caption text). (2) VE-Safety, a benchmark of 440 human-curated real-world technical-image/text instances across 15 safety categories, positioned as the first dataset systematically targeting the Image-as-Basis threat. (3) MM-Plan, an automatic red-teaming framework that reframes multimodal jailbreaking from turn-by-turn reaction to single-pass global plan synthesis, trained via GRPO on a compact open-weight planner (Qwen3-VL-4B) without human-annotated attack trajectories.
threat_model: Black-box, multi-turn adversarial red teaming of frontier MLLMs. The attacker is given a harmful goal g and an image I whose visual content is essential to fulfilling g, and must produce a multi-turn conversation trajectory T = {(i_n, q_n, r_n)} that induces the victim model M_target to emit a response that both satisfies the harmful goal and is 'visually grounded actionable' (specific executable instructions derived from the image, not generic theory). At each turn the attacker may apply a deterministic image operation (e.g., crop/blur a bounding box) plus a text query. The attacker has no gradient access to victim (black-box API), no ground-truth successful-jailbreak corpus, and uses only a compact trainable open-weight planner. Key claimed novelty of the threat: standard text-centric defenses (OCR filters, caption screening, adversarial denoising, static prompt guardrails, single-turn input classifiers) are structurally ineffective because no harmful payload exists in text or noise form; harm is intrinsic to reasoning over the clean visual signal and is decomposed across turns so individual steps appear benign.
limitations: (1) ASR is measured entirely by an LLM judge (Claude 4.5 Sonnet); although validated against 9-annotator human consensus (92.3%/88.5% agreement) and cross-checked with GPT-4o/Gemini judges (>97% verdict overlap), the metric is still model-defined rather than a demonstrated real-world harm. (2) Evaluation is primarily on the authors' own 440-instance VE-Safety benchmark (only 320 test instances; HarmBench-MM results relegated to appendix), so external validity is limited. (3) The attack requires a trainable open-weight planner and per-target judge queries; it is a diagnostic tool, not shown against deployed production defense stacks (built-in commercial safety systems are 'powerful but undisclosed'). (4) The paper proposes NO defense; the 'Defenses and Limitations' section only argues existing guardrails (AdaShield, OmniGuard, JailDAM, Llama Guard 3 Vision, VLGuard) are inadequate against VE, without offering or evaluating a mitigation. (5) Dual-use risk is acknowledged; the authors withhold the GRPO-trained planner weights, releasing only the benchmark, eval code/judge prompts, and base architecture. (6) The benchmark is skewed (Illegal Activity 164, Physical Harm 58 of 440 instances), reflecting availability of technical imagery rather than balanced coverage.

datasets_used: VE-Safety (introduced by this paper): 440 human-curated real-world image/text instances across 15 safety categories (from OpenAI/Meta usage policies), split 80 train / 40 val / 320 test; test further stratified into seen queries (N=106) and unseen queries (N=214); skewed toward Illegal Activity (164) and Physical Harm (58)
HarmBench multimodal subset (Mazeika et al. 2024) used as an additional reference evaluation (reported in Appendix D.3)
Stratified human-audit set of 400 attack trajectories (balanced across MM-Plan vs Direct Plan and Qwen3-VL-8B vs Claude 4.5 Sonnet) for judge validation
quantitative_results: ASR on Claude 4.5 Sonnet: MM-Plan 46.3% vs FigStep 24.4% (best baseline) vs Crescendo 18.1% vs Direct Request 8.4%
ASR on GPT-5: MM-Plan 13.8% vs Crescendo 3.1% vs SI-Attack 1.9% vs FigStep 0.6% vs Direct Request 0.6%

ASR (Table 3): Llama-3.2-11B 64.4, InternVL3-8B 65.0, Qwen3-VL-8B 54.4, GPT-4o 36.9, GPT-5 13.8, Sonnet 3.7 27.2, Sonnet 4.5 46.3, Gemini 2.5 Pro 43.8 (all MM-Plan, statistically significant p<=0.05 over 2nd-best)
Overall improvement of 2-5x over search-based and turn-by-turn baselines
Cross-model transfer (Table 4): Qwen3-VL-8B-trained agent -> Claude 4.5 Sonnet 29.7% (3x the 9.7% zero-shot Direct Plan); Claude-trained -> Qwen3-VL-8B 50.6%
Generalization (Table 5): unseen queries 45.3% vs seen 48.1% on Claude 4.5 Sonnet (<4% drop); Qwen3-VL-8B 53.3% unseen vs 56.6% seen

Judge reliability (Table 6): 92.3% agreement with 9-annotator human consensus on Safety Violation (P 93.8 / R 89.5), 88.5% on Actionable Harm (P 89.8 / R 87.4); >97% binary-verdict overlap across Claude 4.5 Sonnet / GPT-4o / Gemini-2.5 judges
Reward ablation (Table 7): binary reward (Exp-1) 30.6/10.3 -> graded (Exp-2) 38.8/25.3 -> +goal penalty 42.5/30.6 -> +progress 47.5/35.6 -> full MM-Plan 54.4/46.3 (Qwen3-VL-8B / Claude 4.5 Sonnet)
Visual action-space ablation: disabling image operations drops ASR by 18.4% (Qwen3-VL-8B) and 27.5% (Claude 4.5 Sonnet)
Attacker scale (Table 8): scaling trainable planner 4B->8B adds +6.9% ASR; proprietary planners (GPT-5, Claude 4.5 Sonnet) yield near-zero ASR due to refusals (320/320 refusals for GPT-5)
MM-Plan reaches 46.3% vs Crescendo 18.1% on Claude 4.5 Sonnet using nearly half the conversation length
Training config: Qwen3-VL-4B planner, GRPO 10 epochs, group size K=4, N_max=10 turns, alpha=0.1, beta=0.5
baselines_compared: SSA / Safety Snowball Agent (Cui et al. 2025, multi-turn visual context accumulation)
Crescendo (Russinovich et al. 2025, multi-turn linguistic escalation, text-only)
UMK (Wang et al. 2024b) noted but omitted as incompatible with black-box targets
